parts.title: 21. Purification by knowledge and vision of the way

rows.pali: 737.Aṭṭhannaṃ pana ñāṇānaṃ vasena sikhāppattā vipassanā, navamañca saccānulomikañāṇanti ayaṃ paṭipadāñāṇadassanavisuddhi nāma.
rows.english: 1. Now, insight reaches its culmination with the eight knowledges, and knowledge in conformity with truth1 is ninth; these are what is called purification by knowledge and vision of the way.

rows.pali: Aṭṭhannanti cettha upakkilesavimuttaṃ vīthipaṭipannavipassanāsaṅkhātaṃ udayabbayānupassanāñāṇaṃ, bhaṅgānupassanāñāṇaṃ, bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ, ādīnavānupassanāñāṇaṃ, nibbidānupassanāñāṇaṃ, muñcitukamyatāñāṇaṃ, paṭisaṅkhānupassanāñāṇaṃ, saṅkhārupekkhāñāṇanti imāni aṭṭha ñāṇāni veditabbāni.
rows.english: The eight should be understood as follows: (1) knowledge of contemplation of rise and fall, which is insight free from imperfections and steady on its course, (2) knowledge of contemplation of dissolution, (3) knowledge of appearance as terror, (4) knowledge of contemplation of danger, (5) knowledge of contemplation of dispassion, (6) knowledge of desire for deliverance, (7) knowledge of contemplation of reflection, and (8) knowledge of equanimity about formations.2

rows.pali: Navamaṃ saccānulomikañāṇanti anulomassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: “Knowledge in conformity with truth as ninth” is a term for conformity.

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ sampādetukāmena upakkilesavimuttaṃ udayabbayañāṇaṃ ādiṃ katvā etesu ñāṇesu yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: So one who wants to perfect this should make these kinds of knowledge his task, starting with knowledge of rise and fall free from imperfections.

rows.pali: 738.Puna udayabbayañāṇe yogo kimatthiyoti ce?
rows.english: 2.But why does he again pursue knowledge of rise and fall?

rows.pali: Lakkhaṇasallakkhaṇattho.
rows.english: To observe the [three] characteristics.

rows.pali: Udayabbayañāṇaṃ hi heṭṭhā dasahi upakkilesehi upakkiliṭṭhaṃ hutvā yāthāvasarasato tilakkhaṇaṃ sallakkhetuṃ nāsakkhi.
rows.english: The knowledge of rise and fall already dealt with, being disabled by the ten imperfections, was not capable of observing the three characteristics in their true nature;

rows.pali: Upakkilesavimuttaṃ pana sakkoti.
rows.english: but once freed from imperfections, it is able to do so.

rows.pali: Tasmā lakkhaṇasallakkhaṇatthamettha puna yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: So he should pursue it again here in order to observe the characteristics.

rows.pali: 739.Lakkhaṇāni pana kissa amanasikārā kena paṭicchannattā na upaṭṭhahanti?
rows.english: 3. Now, the characteristics fail to become apparent when something is not given attention and so something conceals them. What is that?

rows.pali: Aniccalakkhaṇaṃ tāva udayabbayānaṃ amanasikārā santatiyā paṭicchannattā na upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: Firstly, the characteristic of impermanence does not become apparent because when rise and fall are not given attention, it is concealed by continuity.

rows.pali: Dukkhalakkhaṇaṃ abhiṇhasampaṭipīḷanassa amanasikārā iriyāpathehi paṭicchannattā na upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: The characteristic of pain does not become apparent because, when continuous oppression is not given attention, it is concealed by the postures.

rows.pali: Anattalakkhaṇaṃ nānādhātuvinibbhogassa amanasikārā ghanena paṭicchannattā na upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: The characteristic of not-self does not become apparent because when resolution into the various elements is not given attention, it is concealed by compactness.

rows.pali: Udayabbayampana pariggahetvā santatiyā vikopitāya aniccalakkhaṇaṃ yāthāvasarasato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: 4. However, when continuity is disrupted by discerning rise and fall, the characteristic of impermanence becomes apparent in its true nature.

rows.pali: Abhiṇhasampaṭipīḷanaṃ manasikatvā iriyāpathe ugghāṭite dukkhalakkhaṇaṃ yāthāvasarasato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: When the postures are exposed by attention to continuous oppression, the characteristic of pain becomes apparent in its true nature.

rows.pali: Nānādhātuyo vinibbhujitvā ghanavinibbhoge kate anattalakkhaṇaṃ yāthāvasarasato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: When the resolution of the compact is effected by resolution into elements, the characteristic of not- self becomes apparent in its true nature.3

rows.pali: 740.Ettha ca aniccaṃ, aniccalakkhaṇaṃ, dukkhaṃ, dukkhalakkhaṇaṃ, anattā, anattalakkhaṇanti ayaṃ vibhāgo veditabbo.
rows.english: 5.And here the following differences should be understood: the impermanent, and the characteristic of impermanence; the painful, and the characteristic of pain; the not-self, and the characteristic of not-self.

rows.pali: Tattha aniccanti khandhapañcakaṃ.
rows.english: 6.Herein, the five aggregates are impermanent.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Uppādavayaññathattabhāvā, hutvā abhāvato vā.
rows.english: Because they rise and fall and change, or because of their non-existence after having been.

rows.pali: Uppādavayaññathattaṃ aniccalakkhaṇaṃ hutvā abhāvasaṅkhāto vā ākāravikāro.
rows.english: Rise and fall and change are the characteristic of impermanence; or mode alteration, in other words, non-existence after having been [is the characteristic of impermanence].4

rows.pali: "Yadaniccaṃ taṃ dukkha"nti (saṃ. ni. 3.15) vacanato pana tadeva khandhapañcakaṃ dukkhaṃ.
rows.english: 7. Those same five aggregates are painful because of the words, “What is impermanent is painful” (S III 22).

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Abhiṇhapaṭipīḷanā, abhiṇhapaṭipīḷanākāro dukkhalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Because of continuous oppression. The mode of being continuously oppressed is the characteristic of pain.

rows.pali: "Yaṃ dukkhaṃ tadanattā"ti (saṃ. ni. 3.15) pana vacanato tadeva khandhapañcakaṃ anattā.
rows.english: 8.Those same five aggregates are not-self because of the words, “What is painful is not-self” (S III 22).

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Avasavattanato, avasavattanākāro anattalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Because there is no exercising of power over them. The mode of insusceptibility to the exercise of power is the characteristic of not- self.

rows.pali: Tayidaṃ sabbampi ayaṃ yogāvacaro upakkilesavimuttena vīthipaṭipannavipassanāsaṅkhātena udayabbayānupassanāñāṇena yāthāvasarasato sallakkheti.
rows.english: 9.The meditator observes all this in its true nature with the knowledge of the contemplation of rise and fall, in other words, with insight free from imperfections and steady on its course.

rows.pali: 741.Tassevaṃ sallakkhetvā punappunaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti rūpārūpadhamme tulayato tīrayato taṃ ñāṇaṃ tikkhaṃ hutvā vahati, saṅkhārā lahuṃ upaṭṭhahanti, ñāṇe tikkhe vahante saṅkhāresu lahuṃ upaṭṭhahantesu uppādaṃ vā ṭhitiṃ vā pavattaṃ vā nimittaṃ vā na sampāpuṇāti.
rows.english: 10. When he repeatedly observes in this way, and examines and investigates material and immaterial states, [to see] that they are impermanent, painful, and not-self, then if his knowledge works keenly, formations quickly become apparent.5 Once his knowledge works keenly and formations quickly become apparent, he no longer extends his mindfulness to their arising or presence or occurrence or sign,

rows.pali: Khayavayabhedanirodheyeva sati santiṭṭhati.
rows.english: but brings it to bear only on their cessation as destruction, fall and breakup.6

rows.pali: Tassa "evaṃ uppajjitvā evaṃ nāma saṅkhāragataṃ nirujjhatī"ti passato etasmiṃ ṭhāne bhaṅgānupassanaṃ nāma vipassanāñāṇaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: 11.When insight knowledge has arisen in him in this way so that he sees how the field of formations, having arisen thus, ceases thus, it is called contemplation of dissolution at that stage,7

rows.pali: Yaṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ –
rows.english: with reference to which it is said:

rows.pali: 742.Tattha ārammaṇapaṭisaṅkhāti yaṃkiñci ārammaṇaṃ paṭisaṅkhāya jānitvā, khayato vayato disvāti attho.
rows.english: 12. Herein, after reflecting on an object is having reflected on, having known, any object; the meaning is, having seen it as liable to destruction and fall.

rows.pali: Bhaṅgānupassane paññāti tassa, ārammaṇaṃ khayato vayato paṭisaṅkhāya uppannassa ñāṇassa bhaṅgaṃ anupassane yā paññā, idaṃ vipassane ñāṇanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Understanding of the contemplation of dissolution: any understanding of the contemplation of the dissolution of the knowledge arisen after reflecting on the object as liable to destruction and fall is called knowledge of insight.

rows.pali: Taṃ kathaṃ hotīti ayaṃ tāva kathetukamyatāpucchāya attho.
rows.english: How has the meaning of a question showing desire to expound.

rows.pali: Tato yathā taṃ hoti, taṃ dassetuṃ "rūpārammaṇatā"tiādi vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 13.Next, in order to show how that comes about, consciousness with materiality as its object, etc., is said.

rows.pali: Tattha rūpārammaṇatā cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjatīti rūpārammaṇaṃ cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjati.
rows.english: Herein, consciousness with materiality as its object arises and dissolves: rūpārammaṇaṃ cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjati [is the equivalent of] rūpārammaṇaṃ cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjati;

rows.pali: Atha vā rūpārammaṇabhāve cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjatīti attho.
rows.english: or the meaning is rūpārammaṇabhāve cittaṃ uppajjitvā bhijjati [alternative grammatical substitution].

rows.pali: Taṃ ārammaṇaṃ paṭisaṅkhāti taṃ rūpārammaṇaṃ paṭisaṅkhāya jānitvā, khayato vayato disvāti attho.
rows.english: Having reflected on that object: having reflected on, having known, that object consisting of materiality; the meaning is, having seen it as liable to destruction and fall.

rows.pali: Tassa cittassa bhaṅgaṃ anupassatīti yena cittena taṃ rūpārammaṇaṃ khayato vayato diṭṭhaṃ, tassa cittassa aparena cittena bhaṅgaṃ anupassatīti attho.
rows.english: He contemplates the dissolution of that consciousness: by means of a subsequent consciousness he contemplates the dissolution of that consciousness with which that object consisting of materiality was seen as liable to destruction and fall.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā "ñātañca ñāṇañca ubhopi vipassatī"ti.
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said: “He sees with insight both the known and the knowledge.”

rows.pali: Ettha ca anupassatīti anu anu passati, anekehi ākārehi punappunaṃ passatīti attho.
rows.english: 14. He contemplates (anupassati): he sees always accordingly (anu anu passati); the meaning is, he sees again and again in various modes.

rows.pali: Tenāha – "anupassatīti kathaṃ anupassati.
rows.english: Hence it is said: “He contemplates”: how does he contemplate?

rows.pali: Aniccato anupassatī"tiādi.
rows.english: He contemplates as impermanent, and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha yasmā bhaṅgo nāma aniccatāya paramā koṭi, tasmā so bhaṅgānupassako yogāvacaro sabbaṃ saṅkhāragataṃ aniccato anupassati, no niccato.
rows.english: 15.Herein, dissolution is the culminating point of impermanence, and so the meditator contemplating dissolution contemplates the whole field of formations as impermanent, not as permanent.8

rows.pali: Tato aniccassa dukkhattā, dukkhassa ca anattattā tadeva dukkhato anupassati, no sukhato.
rows.english: Then, because of the painfulness of what is impermanent and because of the non-existence of self in what is painful, he contemplates that same whole field of formations as painful, not as pleasant,

rows.pali: Anattato anupassati no attato.
rows.english: he contemplates it as not-self, not as self.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana yaṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā, na taṃ abhinanditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 16.But what is impermanent, painful, not-self, is not something to delight in;

rows.pali: Yañca anabhinanditabbaṃ, na tattha rajjitabbaṃ.
rows.english: and what is not something to delight in is not something to arouse greed for;

rows.pali: Tasmā etasmiṃ bhaṅgānupassanānusārena "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti diṭṭhe saṅkhāragate nibbindati, no nandati.
rows.english: consequently, when that field of formations is seen as impermanent, painful, not-self, in accordance with the contemplation of dissolution, then he becomes dispassionate, he does not delight;

rows.pali: Virajjati, no rajjati.
rows.english: he causes fading away of greed, he does not inflame it.

rows.pali: So evaṃ arajjanto lokikeneva tāva ñāṇena rāgaṃ nirodheti, no samudeti.
rows.english: When he does not inflame greed thus, he causes cessation of greed, not its origination, which happens firstly by means of mundane knowledge;9

rows.pali: Samudayaṃ na karotīti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is, he does not cause origination.

rows.pali: Atha vā so evaṃ viratto yathā diṭṭhaṃ saṅkhāragataṃ, tathā adiṭṭhampi anvayañāṇavasena nirodheti, no samudeti.
rows.english: 17.Or alternatively, having thus caused the fading away of greed, and caused the cessation of the seen field of formations, he causes the cessation of the unseen too by means of inferential knowledge, he does not originate it.

rows.pali: Nirodhatova manasikaroti.
rows.english: He gives attention only to its cessation,

rows.pali: Nirodhamevassa passati, no samudayanti attho.
rows.english: he sees only its cessation, not its origin, is the meaning.

rows.pali: So evaṃ paṭipanno paṭinissajjati, no ādiyati.
rows.english: 18. Progressing in this way, he relinquishes, he does not grasp.

rows.pali: Kiṃ vuttaṃ hoti?
rows.english: What is meant?

rows.pali: Ayampi aniccādianupassanā tadaṅgavasena saddhiṃ khandhābhisaṅkhārehi kilesānaṃ pariccajanato, saṅkhatadosadassanena ca tabbiparīte nibbāne tanninnatāya pakkhandanato pariccāgapaṭinissaggo ceva pakkhandanapaṭinissaggo cāti vuccati.
rows.english: [What is meant is that] this contemplation of impermanence, etc., is also called both “relinquishment as giving up” and “relinquishment as entering into” (see Paṭis I 194) because, by substitution of opposite qualities, it gives up defilements along with aggregate producing kamma-formations, and because, by seeing the unsatisfactoriness of what is formed, it also enters into, by inclining towards, Nibbāna, which is the opposite of the formed.

rows.pali: Tasmā tāya samannāgato bhikkhu yathāvuttena nayena kilese pariccajati, nibbāne ca pakkhandati.
rows.english: Therefore the bhikkhu who possesses that [contemplation] gives up defilements and enters into Nibbāna in the way stated,

rows.pali: Nāpi nibbattanavasena kilese ādiyati, na adosadassitāvasena saṅkhatārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: he does not grasp (cling to) defilements by causing rebirth, nor does he grasp (cling to) a formed object through failing to see its unsatisfactoriness.

rows.pali: Tena vuccati "paṭinissajjati no ādiyatī"ti.
rows.english: Hence it was said: he relinquishes, he does not grasp.

rows.pali: 743.Idānissa tehi ñāṇehi yesaṃ dhammānaṃ pahānaṃ hoti, taṃ dassetuṃ "aniccato anupassanto niccasaññaṃ pajahatī"tiādi vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 19.Now, in order to show which states are abandoned by these three kinds of knowledge, contemplating as impermanent, he abandons the perception of permanence, etc., is said.

rows.pali: Tattha nandinti sappītikaṃ taṇhaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, delight is craving accompanied by happiness.

rows.pali: Sesaṃ vuttanayameva.
rows.english: The rest is as already stated.

rows.pali: 744.Gāthāsu pana vatthusaṅkamanāti rūpassa bhaṅgaṃ disvā puna yena cittena bhaṅgo diṭṭho, tassāpi bhaṅgadassanavasena purimavatthuto aññavatthusaṅkamanā.
rows.english: 20.As to the stanzas: the substitution of the object [means that] after seeing the dissolution of materiality, there is the substitution of another object for that first object by seeing the dissolution of the consciousness by which the dissolution [of materiality] was seen.

rows.pali: Paññāya ca vivaṭṭanāti udayaṃ pahāya vaye santiṭṭhanā.
rows.english: Transference of understanding is the abandoning of rise and the specializing in fall.

rows.pali: Āvajjanābalañcevāti rūpassa bhaṅgaṃ disvā puna bhaṅgārammaṇassa cittassa bhaṅgadassanatthaṃ anantarameva āvajjanasamatthatā.
rows.english: The power of adverting is the ability, after seeing the dissolution of materiality, to advert immediately for the purpose of seeing the dissolution of the consciousness that had that dissolution as its object.

rows.pali: Paṭisaṅkhāvipassanāti esā ārammaṇapaṭisaṅkhābhaṅgānupassanā nāma.
rows.english: Are insight following reflection: this is called contemplation of dissolution after reflecting on an object.

rows.pali: 745.Ārammaṇaanvayena ubho ekavavatthanāti paccakkhato diṭṭhassa ārammaṇassa anvayena anugamanena yathā idaṃ, tathā atītepi saṅkhāragataṃ bhijjittha, anāgatepi bhijjissatīti evaṃ ubhinnaṃ ekasabhāveneva vavatthāpananti attho.
rows.english: 21.Defining both to be alike by inference from that same object: the meaning is that by inference, by induction, from the object seen by actual experience he defines both [the seen and the unseen] to have a single individual essence thus, “The field of formations dissolved in the past, and will break up in the future, just as it does [in the present].”

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ porāṇehi –
rows.english: And this is said by the Ancients:

rows.pali: Nirodhe adhimuttatāti evaṃ ubhinnaṃ bhaṅgavasena ekavavatthānaṃ katvā tasmiññeva bhaṅgasaṅkhāte nirodhe adhimuttatā taggarutā tanninnatā tappoṇatā tappabbhāratāti attho.
rows.english: 22.Intentness on cessation: after thus giving to both a single definition based on their dissolution, he thus becomes intent on cessation, in other words, on that same dissolution. The meaning is that he attaches importance to it, inclines, tends, leans towards it.

rows.pali: Vayalakkhaṇavipassanāti esā vayalakkhaṇavipassanā nāmāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Are insight in the mark of fall: what is meant is that this is called insight into the characteristic of fall.

rows.pali: 746.Ārammaṇañca paṭisaṅkhāti purimañca rūpādiārammaṇaṃ jānitvā.
rows.english: 23. Having reflected on the object: having first known the object consisting of materiality, and so on.

rows.pali: Bhaṅgañca anupassatīti tassārammaṇassa bhaṅgaṃ disvā tadārammaṇassa cittassa bhaṅgaṃ anupassati.
rows.english: Dissolution he contemplates: having seen the dissolution of that object, he contemplates the dissolution of the consciousness that had that as its object.

rows.pali: Suññato ca upaṭṭhānanti tassevaṃ bhaṅgaṃ anupassato "saṅkhārāva bhijjanti, tesaṃ bhedo maraṇaṃ, na añño koci atthī"ti suññato upaṭṭhānaṃ ijjhati.
rows.english: 24.Appearance then as empty: while he is contemplating dissolution in this way, he succeeds in making [formations] appear as void thus, “Only formations breakup; their breakup is death; there is nothing else at all10.”

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Adhipaññāvipassanāti yā ca ārammaṇapaṭisaṅkhā yā ca bhaṅgānupassanā yañca suññato upaṭṭhānaṃ, ayaṃ adhipaññāvipassanā nāmāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 25.Is insight of higher understanding: what is meant is that the reflection on the object, the contemplation of dissolution, and the appearance as void are called insight of higher understanding.

rows.pali: 747.Kusalo tīsu anupassanāsūti aniccānupassanādīsu tīsu cheko bhikkhu.
rows.english: 26.Skilled in the three contemplations: a bhikkhu who is competent in the three beginning with contemplation of impermanence.

rows.pali: Catasso ca vipassanāsūti nibbidādīsu ca catūsu vipassanāsu.
rows.english: And in the fourfold insight too: in the four kinds of insight beginning with dispassion.

rows.pali: Tayo upaṭṭhāne kusalatāti khayato vayato suññatoti imasmiñca tividhe upaṭṭhāne kusalatāya.
rows.english: Skilled in the three appearances: and owing to skill in this threefold appearance, namely, as liable to destruction and fall, as terror, and as void.12

rows.pali: Nānādiṭṭhīsu na kampatīti sassatadiṭṭhiādīsu nānappakārāsu diṭṭhīsu na vedhati.
rows.english: The various views will shake him not: he does not vacillate on account of the various kinds of views such as the eternity view.

rows.pali: 748.So evaṃ avedhamāno "aniruddhameva nirujjhati, abhinnameva bhijjatī"ti pavattamanasikāro dubbalabhājanassa viya bhijjamānassa, sukhumarajasseva vippakiriyamānassa, tilānaṃ viya bhajjiyamānānaṃ sabbasaṅkhārānaṃ uppādaṭṭhitipavattanimittaṃ vissajjetvā bhedameva passati.
rows.english: 27. When he no longer vacillates and so constantly bears in mind that the unceased will also cease, the undissolved will also dissolve, then he disregards the arising, presence, occurrence and sign of all formations, which keep on breaking up, like fragile pottery being smashed, like fine dust being dispersed, like sesame seeds being roasted, and he sees only their breakup.

rows.pali: So yathā nāma cakkhumā puriso pokkharaṇītīre vā nadītīre vā ṭhito thūlaphusitake deve vassante udakapiṭṭhe mahantamahantāni udakabubbuḷakāni uppajjitvā uppajjitvā sīghaṃ sīghaṃ bhijjamānāni passeyya, evameva sabbe saṅkhārā bhijjanti bhijjantīti passati.
rows.english: Just as a man with eyes standing on the bank of a pond or on the bank of a river during heavy rain would see large bubbles appearing on the surface of the water and breaking up as soon as they appeared, so too he sees how formations break up all the time.

rows.pali: Evarūpaṃ hi yogāvacaraṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: The Blessed One said of such a meditator:

rows.pali: 749.Tassevaṃ "sabbe saṅkhārā bhijjanti bhijjantī"ti abhiṇhaṃ passato aṭṭhānisaṃsaparivāraṃ bhaṅgānupassanāñāṇaṃ balappattaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 28.When he constantly sees that all formations thus break up all the time, then contemplation of dissolution grows strong in him,

rows.pali: Tatrime aṭṭhānisaṃsā – bhavadiṭṭhippahānaṃ, jīvitanikantipariccāgo, sadāyuttapayuttatā, visuddhājīvitā, ussukkappahānaṃ, vigatabhayatā, khantisoraccapaṭilābho, aratiratisahanatāti.
rows.english: bringing eight advantages, which are these: abandoning of [false] view of becoming, giving up attachment to life, constant application, a purified livelihood, no more anxiety, absence of fear, acquisition of patience and gentleness, and conquest of aversion (boredom) and sensual delight.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: 750.Tassevaṃ sabbasaṅkhārānaṃ khayavayabhedanirodhārammaṇaṃ bhaṅgānupassanaṃ āsevantassa bhāventassa bahulīkarontassa sabbabhavayonigatiṭhitisattāvāsesu pabhedakā saṅkhārā sukhena jīvitukāmassa bhīrukapurisassa sīhabyagghadīpiacchataracchayakkharakkhasacaṇḍagoṇacaṇḍakukkurapabhinnamada-caṇḍahatthighoraāsīvisaasanivicakkasusānaraṇabhūmijalitaaṅgārakāsuādayo viya mahābhayaṃ hutvā upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: 29. As he repeats, develops and cultivates in this way the contemplation of dissolution, the object of which is cessation consisting in the destruction, fall and breakup of all formations, then formations classed according to all kinds of becoming, generation, destiny, station, or abode of beings, appear to him in the form of a great terror, as lions, tigers, leopards, bears, hyenas, spirits, ogres, fierce bulls, savage dogs, rut-maddened wild elephants, hideous venomous serpents, thunderbolts, charnel grounds, battlefields, flaming coal pits, etc., appear to a timid man who wants to live in peace.

rows.pali: Tassa "atītā saṅkhārā niruddhā, paccuppannā nirujjhanti, anāgate nibbattanakasaṅkhārāpi evameva nirujjhissantī"ti passato etasmiṃ ṭhāne bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ nāma uppajjati.
rows.english: When he sees how past formations have ceased, present ones are ceasing, and those to be generated in the future will cease in just the same way, then what is called knowledge of appearance as terror arises in him at that stage.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ upamā – ekissā kira itthiyā tayo puttā rājaparādhikā, tesaṃ rājā sīsacchedaṃ āṇāpesi.
rows.english: 30. Here is a simile: a woman’s three sons had offended against the king, it seems. The king ordered their heads to be cut off.

rows.pali: Sā puttehi saddhiṃ āghātanaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: She went with her sons to the place of their execution.

rows.pali: Athassā jeṭṭhaputtassa sīsaṃ chinditvā majjhimassa chindituṃ ārabhiṃsu.
rows.english: When they had cut off the eldest one’s head, they set about cutting off the middle one’s head.

rows.pali: Sā jeṭṭhassa sīsaṃ chinnaṃ majjhimassa ca chijjamānaṃ disvā kaniṭṭhamhi ālayaṃ vissajji "ayampi etesaññeva sadiso bhavissatī"ti.
rows.english: Seeing the eldest one’s head already cut off and the middle one’s head being cut off, she gave up hope for the youngest, thinking, “He too will fare like them.”

rows.pali: Tattha tassā itthiyā jeṭṭhaputtassa chinnasīsadassanaṃ viya yogino atītasaṅkhārānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ, majjhimassa chijjamānasīsadassanaṃ viya paccuppannānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ, "ayampi etesaññeva sadiso bhavissatī"ti kaniṭṭhaputtamhi ālayavissajjanaṃ viya "anāgatepi nibbattanakasaṅkhārā bhijjissantī"ti anāgatānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ.
rows.english: Now, the meditator’s seeing the cessation of past formations is like the woman’s seeing the eldest son’s head cut off. His seeing the cessation of those present is like her seeing the middle one’s head being cut off. His seeing the cessation of those in the future, thinking, “Formations to be generated in the future will cease too,” is like her giving up hope for the youngest son, thinking, “He too will fare like them.”

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ passato etasmiṃ ṭhāne uppajjati bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ.
rows.english: When he sees in this way, knowledge of appearance as terror arises in him at that stage.

rows.pali: Aparāpi upamā – ekā kira pūtipajā itthī dasa dārake vijāyi.
rows.english: 31.Also another simile: a woman with an infected womb had, it seems, given birth to ten children.

rows.pali: Tesu nava matā, eko hatthagato marati, aparo kucchiyaṃ.
rows.english: Of these, nine had already died and one was dying in her hands. There was another in her womb.

rows.pali: Sā nava dārake mate dasamañca mīyamānaṃ disvā kucchigate ālayaṃ vissajji "ayampi etesaññeva sadiso bhavissatī"ti.
rows.english: Seeing that nine were dead and the tenth was dying, she gave up hope about the one in her womb, thinking, “It too will fare just like them.”

rows.pali: Tattha tassā itthiyā navannaṃ dārakānaṃ maraṇānussaraṇaṃ viya yogino atītasaṅkhārānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ, hatthagatassa mīyamānabhāvadassanaṃ viya yogino paccuppannānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ, kucchigate ālayavissajjanaṃ viya anāgatānaṃ nirodhadassanaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, the meditator’s seeing the cessation of past formations is like the woman’s remembering the death of the nine children. The meditator’s seeing the cessation of those present is like her seeing the moribund state of the one in her hands. His seeing the cessation of those in the future is like her giving up hope about the one in her womb.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ passato etasmiṃ khaṇe uppajjati bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ.
rows.english: When he sees in this way, knowledge of appearance as terror arises in him at that stage.

rows.pali: 751.Bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ pana bhāyati na bhāyatīti?
rows.english: 32.But does the knowledge of appearance as terror [itself] fear or does it not fear?

rows.pali: Na bhāyati.
rows.english: It does not fear.

rows.pali: Tañhi atītā saṅkhārā niruddhā, paccuppannā nirujjhanti, anāgatā nirujjhissantīti tīraṇamattameva hoti.
rows.english: For it is simply the mere judgment that past formations have ceased, present ones are ceasing, and future ones will cease.

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā nāma cakkhumā puriso nagaradvāre tisso aṅgārakāsuyo olokayamāno sayaṃ na bhāyati, kevalaṃ hissa "ye ye ettha nipatissanti, sabbe anappakaṃ dukkhamanubhavissantī"ti tīraṇamattameva hoti.
rows.english: Just as a man with eyes looking at three charcoal pits at a city gate is not himself afraid, since he only forms the mere judgment that all who fall into them will suffer no little pain;

rows.pali: Yathā vā pana cakkhumā puriso khadirasūlaṃ ayosūlaṃ suvaṇṇasūlanti paṭipāṭiyā ṭhapitaṃ sūlattayaṃ olokayamāno sayaṃ na bhāyati, kevalaṃ hissa "ye ye imesu sūlesu nipatissanti, sabbe anappakaṃ dukkhamanubhavissantī"ti tīraṇamattameva hoti, evameva bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ sayaṃ na bhāyati, kevalaṃ hissa aṅgārakāsuttayasadisesu, sūlattayasadisesu ca tīsu bhavesu "atītā saṅkhārā niruddhā, paccuppannā nirujjhanti, anāgatā nirujjhissantī"ti tīraṇamattameva hoti.
rows.english: —or just as when a man with eyes looks at three spikes set in a row, an acacia spike, an iron spike, and a gold spike, he is not himself afraid, since he only forms the mere judgment that all who fall on these spikes will suffer no little pain;—so too the knowledge of appearance as terror does not itself fear; it only forms the mere judgment that in the three kinds of becoming, which resemble the three charcoal pits and the three spikes, past formations have ceased, present ones are ceasing, and future ones will cease.

rows.pali: Yasmā panassa kevalaṃ sabbabhavayonigatiṭhitinivāsagatā saṅkhārā byasanāpannā sappaṭibhayā hutvā bhayato upaṭṭhahanti, tasmā bhayatupaṭṭhānanti vuccati.
rows.english: 33.But it is called “appearance as terror” only because formations in all kinds of becoming, generation, destiny, station, or abode are fearful in being bound for destruction and so they appear only as a terror.

rows.pali: Evaṃ bhayato upaṭṭhāne panassa ayaṃ pāḷi –
rows.english: Here is the text about its appearance to him as terror:

rows.pali: Tattha nimittanti saṅkhāranimittaṃ.
rows.english: 34. Herein, the sign is the sign of formations.

rows.pali: Atītānāgatapaccuppannānaṃ saṅkhārānamevetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: This is a term for past, future and present formations themselves.

rows.pali: Aniccato manasikaronto hi saṅkhārānaṃ maraṇameva passati, tenassa nimittaṃ bhayato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: He sees only the death of formations when he brings them to mind as impermanent and so the sign appears to him as a terror.

rows.pali: Pavattanti rūpārūpabhavapavatti.
rows.english: Occurrence is occurrence in material and immaterial becoming.

rows.pali: Dukkhato manasikaronto hi sukhasammatāyapi pavattiyā abhiṇhapaṭipīḷanabhāvameva passati, tenassa pavattaṃ bhayato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: He sees occurrence—though ordinarily reckoned as pleasure—only as a state of being continuously oppressed when he brings them to mind as painful, and so occurrence appears to him as a terror.

rows.pali: Anattato manasikaronto pana ubhayampetaṃ suññagāmaṃ viya marīcigandhabbanagarādīni viya ca rittaṃ tucchaṃ suññaṃ assāmikaṃ apariṇāyakaṃ passati.
rows.english: He sees both the sign and the occurrence as empty, vain, void, without power or guide, like an empty village, a mirage, a goblin city, etc., when he brings [them] to mind as not-self,

rows.pali: Tenassa nimittañca pavattañca ubhayaṃ bhayato upaṭṭhātīti.
rows.english: and so the sign and occurrence appear to him as a terror.

rows.pali: 752.Tassa taṃ bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ āsevantassa bhāventassa bahulīkarontassa sabbabhavayonigatiṭhitisattāvāsesu neva tāṇaṃ, na leṇaṃ, na gati, nappaṭisaraṇaṃ paññāyati.
rows.english: 35. As he repeats, develops and cultivates the knowledge of appearance as terror he finds no asylum, no shelter, no place to go to, no refuge in any kind of becoming, generation, destiny, station, or abode.

rows.pali: Sabbabhavayonigatiṭhitinivāsagatesu saṅkhāresu ekasaṅkhārepi patthanā vā parāmāso vā na hoti.
rows.english: In all the kinds of becoming, generation, destiny, station, and abode there is not a single formation that he can place his hopes in or hold on to.

rows.pali: Tayo bhavā vītaccikaṅgārapuṇṇaaṅgārakāsuyo viya, cattāro mahābhūtā ghoravisaāsīvisā viya, pañcakkhandhā ukkhittāsikavadhakā viya, cha ajjhattikāyatanāni suññagāmo viya, cha bāhirāyatanāni gāmaghātakacorā viya, satta viññāṇaṭṭhitiyo, nava ca sattāvāsā ekādasahi aggīhi ādittā sampajjalitā sajotibhūtā viya ca, sabbe saṅkhārā gaṇḍabhūtā rogabhūtā sallabhūtā aghabhūtā ābādhabhūtā viya ca nirassādā nirasā mahāādīnavarāsibhūtā hutvā upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: The three kinds of becoming appear like charcoal pits full of glowing coals, the four primary elements like hideous venomous snakes (S IV 174), the five aggregates like murderers with raised weapons (S IV 174), the six internal bases like an empty village, the six external bases like village-raiding robbers (S IV 174–75), the seven stations of consciousness and the nine abodes of beings as though burning, blazing and glowing with the eleven fires (see S IV 19), and all formations appear as a huge mass of dangers destitute of satisfaction or substance, like a tumour, a disease, a dart, a calamity, an affliction (see M I 436).

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Sukhena jīvitukāmassa bhīrukapurisassa ramaṇīyākārasaṇṭhitampi savāḷakamiva vanagahanaṃ, sasaddūlā viya guhā, sagāharakkhasaṃ viya udakaṃ, samussitakhaggā viya paccatthikā, savisaṃ viya bhojanaṃ, sacoro viya maggo, ādittamiva agāraṃ, uyyuttasenā viya raṇabhūmi.
rows.english: 36.They appear as a forest thicket of seemingly pleasant aspect but infested with wild beasts, a cave full of tigers, water haunted by monsters and ogres, an enemy with raised sword, poisoned food, a road beset by robbers, a burning coal, a battlefield between contending armies appear to a timid man who wants to live in peace.

rows.pali: Yathā hi so puriso etāni savāḷakavanagahanādīni āgamma bhīto saṃviggo lomahaṭṭhajāto samantato ādīnavameva passati, evamevāyaṃ yogāvacaro bhaṅgānupassanāvasena sabbasaṅkhāresu bhayato upaṭṭhitesu samantato nirasaṃ nirassādaṃ ādīnavameva passati.
rows.english: And just as that man is frightened and horrified and his hair stands up when he comes upon a thicket infested by wild beasts, etc., and he sees it as nothing but danger, so too when all formations have appeared as a terror by contemplation of dissolution, this meditator sees them as utterly destitute of any core or any satisfaction and as nothing but danger.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ passato ādīnavañāṇaṃ nāma uppannaṃ hoti.
Yaṃ sandhāya idaṃ vuttaṃ –
753.Tattha uppādoti purimakammapaccayā idha uppatti.
rows.english: 38.Herein, arising is appearance here [in this becoming] with previous kamma as condition.

rows.pali: Pavattanti tathā uppannassa pavatti.
rows.english: Occurrence is the occurrence of what has arisen in this way.

rows.pali: Nimittanti sabbampi saṅkhāranimittaṃ.
rows.english: The sign is the sign of all formations.

rows.pali: Āyūhanāti āyatiṃ paṭisandhihetubhūtaṃ kammaṃ.
rows.english: Accumulation is the kamma that is the cause of future rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Paṭisandhīti āyatiṃ uppatti.
rows.english: Rebirth-linking is future appearance.

rows.pali: Gatīti yāya gatiyā sā paṭisandhi hoti.
rows.english: Destiny is the destiny in which the rebirth-linking takes place.

rows.pali: Nibbattīti khandhānaṃ nibbattanaṃ.
rows.english: Generation is the generating of aggregates.

rows.pali: Upapattīti "samāpannassa vā upapannassa vā"ti (dha. sa. 1289, 1291) evaṃ vuttā vipākappavatti.
rows.english: Re-arising is the occurrence of kamma-result stated thus, “In one who has attained [to it] or in one who has been reborn [in it]” (Dhs §1282).

rows.pali: Jātīti jarādīnaṃ paccayabhūtā bhavapaccayā jāti.
rows.english: Birth is birth with becoming as its condition, itself a condition for ageing and so on.

rows.pali: Jarāmaraṇādayo pākaṭā eva.
rows.english: Ageing, sickness, death, etc., are obvious.

rows.pali: Ettha ca uppādādayo pañceva ādīnavañāṇassa vatthuvasena vuttā.
rows.english: 39. And here only the five beginning with arising are mentioned as actual objects of knowledge of danger;

rows.pali: Sesā tesaṃ vevacanavasena.
rows.english: the rest are synonyms for them.

rows.pali: Nibbatti jātīti idañhi dvayaṃ uppādassa ceva paṭisandhiyā ca vevacanaṃ.
rows.english: For the pair, generation and birth, are synonyms both for arising and for rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Gati upapattīti idaṃ dvayaṃ pavattassa.
rows.english: The pair, destiny and re-arising, are synonyms for occurrence.

rows.pali: Jarādayo nimittassāti.
rows.english: Ageing, etc., are synonyms for the sign.

rows.pali: Tenāha –
rows.english: Hence it was said:

rows.pali: Anuppādo khemanti santipade ñāṇantiādi pana ādīnavañāṇassa paṭipakkhañāṇadassanatthaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 40.Knowledge of the state of peace is this: “Non-arising is safety,” etc.: this, however, should be understood as said for the purpose of showing the opposite kind of knowledge to knowledge of danger.

rows.pali: Bhayatupaṭṭhānena vā ādīnavaṃ disvā ubbiggahadayānaṃ abhayampi atthi khemaṃ nirādīnavanti assāsajananatthampi etaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Or when it is stated in this way, that there is safety without terror and free from danger, it is for the purpose of comforting those who are upset in their hearts by seeing danger through appearance as terror.

rows.pali: Yasmā vā panassa uppādādayo bhayato sūpaṭṭhitā honti, tassa tappaṭipakkhaninnaṃ cittaṃ hoti, tasmā bhayatupaṭṭhānavasena siddhassa ādīnavañāṇassa ānisaṃsadassanatthampetaṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Or else, when arising, etc., have clearly appeared to a man as terror, his mind inclines towards their opposites, and so this is said for the purpose of showing the advantages in the knowledge of danger established by the appearance as terror.

rows.pali: Ettha ca yaṃ bhayaṃ, taṃ yasmā niyamato dukkhaṃ.
rows.english: 41. And here (1.a.) what is terror is certainly (2.a) suffering,

rows.pali: Taṃ vaṭṭāmisalokāmisakilesāmisehi avippamuttattā sāmisameva.
rows.english: and what is suffering is purely (3.a.) worldly since it is not free from the worldliness of the rounds [of becoming], of the world, and of defilements,13

rows.pali: Yañca sāmisaṃ, taṃ saṅkhāramattameva.
rows.english: and what is worldly consists solely of (4.a) formations.

rows.pali: Tasmā "uppādo dukkhanti bhayatupaṭṭhāne paññā ādīnave ñāṇa"ntiādi vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore it is said that (2.a) understanding of appearance as terror thus, “Arising is suffering,” is knowledge of danger, and so on.

rows.pali: Evaṃ santepi bhayākārena dukkhākārena sāmisākārenāti evaṃ ākāranānattato pavattivasenevettha nānattaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: And while this is so, still there is a difference to be understood here in the way these things [beginning with “arising”] occur, since there is a difference in their mode with the mode of terror, the mode of suffering, and the mode of worldliness.

rows.pali: Dasañāṇe pajānātīti ādīnavañāṇaṃ pajānanto uppādādivatthukāni pañca, anuppādādivatthukāni pañcāti dasa ñāṇāni pajānāti paṭivijjhati sacchikaroti.
rows.english: 42.Ten knowledges he understands: one who understands knowledge of danger understands, penetrates, realizes, ten kinds of knowledge, that is, the five based on arising, etc., and the five on non-arising and so on.

rows.pali: Dvinnaṃ ñāṇānaṃ kusalatāti ādīnavañāṇassa ceva santipadañāṇassa cāti imesaṃ dvinnaṃ kusalatāya.
rows.english: When skilled in these two kinds of knowledge: with skill in the two, that is, knowledge of danger and knowledge of the state of peace.

rows.pali: Nānādiṭṭhīsuna kampatīti paramadiṭṭhadhammanibbānādivasena pavattāsu diṭṭhīsu na vedhati.
rows.english: The various views will shake him not: he does not vacillate about views that occur such as “The ultimate Nibbāna is here and now.”

rows.pali: Sesamettha uttānamevāti.
rows.english: The rest is clear.

rows.pali: 754.So evaṃ sabbasaṅkhāre ādīnavato passanto sabbabhavayonigativiññāṇaṭṭhitisattāvāsagate sabhedake saṅkhāragate nibbindati ukkaṇṭhati nābhiramati.
rows.english: 43. When he sees all formations in this way as danger, he becomes dispassionate towards, is dissatisfied with, takes no delight in the manifold field of formations belonging to any kind of becoming, destiny, station of consciousness, or abode of beings.

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma, cittakūṭapabbatapādābhirato suvaṇṇarājahaṃso asucimhi caṇḍālagāmadvāraāvāṭe nābhiramati, sattasu mahāsaresuyeva abhiramati, evameva ayampi yogīrājahaṃso suparidiṭṭhādīnave sabhedake saṅkhāragate nābhiramati.
rows.english: Just as a golden swan that loves the foothills of Citta Peak finds delight, not in a filthy puddle at the gate of a village of outcastes, but only in the seven great lakes (see XIII.38), so too this meditator swan finds delight, not in the manifold formations seen clearly as danger,

rows.pali: Bhāvanārāmatāya pana bhāvanāratiyā samannāgatattā sattasu anupassanāsuyeva ramati.
rows.english: but only in the seven contemplations, because he delights in development.

rows.pali: Yathā ca suvaṇṇapañjare pakkhitto sīho migarājā nābhiramati, tiyojanasahassavitthate pana himavanteyeva ramati, evamayaṃ yogīsīho tividhe sugatibhavepi nābhiramati, tīsu pana anupassanāsuyeva ramati.
rows.english: And just as the lion, king of beasts, finds delight, not when put into a gold cage, but only in Himalaya with its three thousand leagues’ extent, so too the meditator lion finds delight, not in the triple becoming of the happy destiny,14 but only in the three contemplations.

rows.pali: Yathā ca sabbaseto sattapatiṭṭho iddhimā vehāsaṅgamo chaddanto nāgarājā nagaramajjhe nābhiramati, himavati chaddantadahagahaneyeva abhiramati, evamayaṃ yogīvaravāraṇo sabbasmimpi saṅkhāragate nābhiramati, anuppādo khemantiādinā nayena diṭṭhe santipadeyeva abhiramati, tanninnatappoṇatappabbhāramānaso hotīti.
rows.english: And just as Chaddanta, king of elephants, all white with sevenfold stance, possessed of supernormal power, who travels through the air,15 finds pleasure, not in the midst of a town, but only in the Chaddanta Lake and Wood in the Himalaya, so too this meditator elephant finds delight, not in any formation, but only in the state of peace seen in the way beginning “Non-arising is safety,” and his mind tends, inclines, and leans towards that.

rows.pali: 755.Taṃ panetaṃ purimena ñāṇadvayena atthato ekaṃ.
rows.english: 44.[Knowledge of contemplation of danger] is the same as the last two kinds of knowledge in meaning.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: "Bhayatupaṭṭhānaṃ ekameva tīṇi nāmāni labhati, sabbasaṅkhāre bhayato addasāti bhayatupaṭṭhānaṃ nāma jātaṃ.
rows.english: “Knowledge of appearance as terror while one only has three names: It saw all formations as terror, thus the name ‘appearance as terror’ arose;

rows.pali: Tesuyeva saṅkhāresu ādīnavaṃ uppādetīti ādīnavānupassanā nāma jātaṃ.
rows.english: it aroused the [appearance of] danger in those same formations, thus the name ‘contemplation of danger’ arose;

rows.pali: Tesuyeva saṅkhāresu nibbindamānaṃ uppannanti nibbidānupassanā nāma jāta"nti.
rows.english: it arose, becoming dispassionate towards those same formations, thus the name ‘contemplation of dispassion’ arose.”

rows.pali: Pāḷiyampi vuttaṃ – "yā ca bhayatupaṭṭhāne paññā, yañca ādīnave ñāṇaṃ, yā ca nibbidā, ime dhammā ekatthā, byañjanameva nāna"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.227).
rows.english: Also it is said in the text: “Understanding of appearance as terror, knowledge of danger, and dispassion: these things are one in meaning, only the letter is different” (Paṭis II 63).

rows.pali: 756.Iminā pana nibbidāñāṇena imassa kulaputtassa nibbindantassa ukkaṇṭhantassa anabhiramantassa sabbabhavayonigativiññāṇaṭṭhitisattāvāsagatesu sabhedakesu saṅkhāresu ekasaṅkhārepi cittaṃ na sajjati, na laggati, na bajjhati, sabbasmā saṅkhāragatā muccitukāmaṃ nissaritukāmaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 45. When, owing to this knowledge of dispassion, this clansman becomes dispassionate towards, is dissatisfied with, takes no delight in any single one of all the manifold formations in any kind of becoming, generation, destiny, station of consciousness, or abode of beings, his mind no longer sticks fast, cleaves, fastens on to them, and he becomes desirous of being delivered from the whole field of formations and escaping from it.

rows.pali: Yathā kiṃ?
rows.english: Like what?

rows.pali: Yathā nāma jālabbhantaragato maccho, sappamukhagato maṇḍūko, pañjarapakkhitto vanakukkuṭo, daḷhapāsavasagato migo, ahituṇḍikahatthagato sappo, mahāpaṅkapakkhando kuñjaro, supaṇṇamukhagato nāgarājā, rāhumukhappaviṭṭho cando, sapattaparivārito purisoti evamādayo tato tato muccitukāmā nissaritukāmāva honti, evaṃ tassa yogino cittaṃ sabbasmā saṅkhāragatā muccitukāmaṃ nissaritukāmaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 46.Just as a fish in a net, a frog in a snake’s jaws, a jungle fowl shut into a cage, a deer fallen into the clutches of a strong snare, a snake in the hands of a snake charmer, an elephant stuck fast in a great bog, a royal nāga in the mouth of a supaṇṇa, the moon inside Rāhu’s mouth,16 a man encircled by enemies, etc.— just as these are desirous of being delivered, of finding an escape from these things, so too this meditator’s mind is desirous of being delivered from the whole field of formations and escaping from it.

rows.pali: Athassa evaṃ sabbasaṅkhāresu vigatālayassa sabbasmā saṅkhāragatā muccitukāmassa uppajjati muñcitukamyatā ñāṇanti.
rows.english: Then, when he thus no longer relies on any formations and is desirous of being delivered from the whole field of formations, knowledge of desire for deliverance arises in him.

rows.pali: 757.So evaṃ sabbabhavayonigatiṭṭhitinivāsagatehi sabhedakehi saṅkhārehi muccitukāmo sabbasmā saṅkhāragatā muccituṃ puna te evaṃ saṅkhāre paṭisaṅkhānupassanāñāṇena tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: 47.Being thus desirous of deliverance from all the manifold formations in any kind of becoming, generation, destiny, station, or abode, in order to be delivered from the whole field of formations he again discerns those same formations, attributing to them the three characteristics by knowledge of contemplation of reflection.

rows.pali: So sabbasaṅkhāre anaccantikato, tāvakālikato, uppādavayaparicchinnato, palokato, calato, pabhaṅguto, addhuvato, vipariṇāmadhammato, assārakato, vibhavato, saṅkhatato, maraṇadhammatotiādīhi kāraṇehi aniccāti passati.
rows.english: 48.He sees all formations as impermanent for the following reasons: because they are non-continuous, temporary, limited by rise and fall, disintegrating, fickle, perishable, unenduring, subject to change, coreless, due to be annihilated, formed, subject to death, and so on.

rows.pali: Abhiṇhapaṭipīḷanato, dukkhamato, dukkhavatthuto, rogato, gaṇḍato, sallato, aghato, ābādhato, ītito, upaddavato, bhayato, upasaggato, atāṇato, aleṇato, asaraṇato, ādīnavato, aghamūlato, vadhakato, sāsavato, mārāmisato, jātidhammato, jarādhammato, byādhidhammato, sokadhammato, paridevadhammato, upāyāsadhammato, saṃkilesikadhammatotiādīhi kāraṇehi dukkhāti passati.
rows.english: He sees them as painful for the following reasons: because they are continuously oppressed, hard to bear, the basis of pain, a disease, a tumour, a dart, a calamity, an affliction, a plague, a disaster, a terror, a menace, no protection, no shelter, no refuge, a danger, the root of calamity, murderous, subject to cankers, Māra’s bait, subject to birth, subject to ageing, subject to illness, subject to sorrow, subject to lamentation, subject to despair, subject to defilement, and so on.

rows.pali: Ajaññato, duggandhato, jegucchato, paṭikkūlato, amaṇḍanārahato, virūpato, bībhacchatotiādīhi kāraṇehi dukkhalakkhaṇassa parivārabhūtato asubhato passati.
rows.english: He sees all formations as foul (ugly)—the ancillary characteristic to that of pain—for the following reasons: because they are objectionable, stinking, disgusting, repulsive, unaffected by disguise, hideous, loathsome, and so on.

rows.pali: Parato, rittato, tucchato, suññato, assāmikato, anissarato, avasavattitotiādīhi kāraṇehi anattato passati.
rows.english: He sees all formations as not-self for the following reasons: because they are alien, empty, vain, void, ownerless, with no Overlord, with none to wield power over them, and so on.

rows.pali: 758.Evañhi passatānena tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā saṅkhārā pariggahitā nāma honti.
rows.english: It is when he sees formations in this way that he is said to discern them by attributing to them the three characteristics.

rows.pali: Kasmā panāyamete evaṃ pariggaṇhātīti?
rows.english: 49.But why does he discern them in this way?

rows.pali: Muñcanassa upāyasampādanatthaṃ.
rows.english: In order to contrive the means to deliverance.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ upamā – eko kira puriso "macche gahessāmī"ti macchakhippaṃ gahetvā udake oḍḍāpesi so khippamukhena hatthaṃ otāretvā antoudake sappaṃ gīvāya gahetvā "maccho me gahito"ti attamano ahosi.
rows.english: Here is a simile: a man thought to catch a fish, it seems, so he took a fishing net and cast it in the water. He put his hand into the mouth of the net under the water and seized a snake by the neck. He was glad, thinking, “I have caught a fish.”

rows.pali: So "mahā vata mayā maccho laddho"ti ukkhipitvā passanto sovatthikattayadassanena sappoti sañjānitvā bhīto ādīnavaṃ disvā gahaṇe nibbinno muñcitukāmo hutvā muñcanassa upāyaṃ karonto agganaṅguṭṭhato paṭṭhāya hatthaṃ nibbeṭhetvā bāhuṃ ukkhipitvā uparisīse dve tayo vāre āvijjhitvā sappaṃ dubbalaṃ katvā "gaccha duṭṭha sappā"ti nissajjitvā vegena taḷākapāḷiṃ āruyha "mahantassa vata bho sappassa mukhato muttomhī"ti āgatamaggaṃ olokayamāno aṭṭhāsi.
rows.english: In the belief that he had caught a big fish, he lifted it up to see. When he saw three marks, he perceived that it was a snake and he was terrified. He saw danger, felt dispassion (revulsion) for what he had seized, and desired to be delivered from it. Contriving a means to deliverance, he unwrapped [the coils from] his hand, starting from the tip of its tail. Then he raised his arm, and when he had weakened the snake by swinging it two or three times round his head, he flung it away, crying “Go, foul snake.” Then quickly scrambling up on to dry land, he stood looking back whence he had come, thinking, “Goodness, I have been delivered from the jaws of a huge snake! ”

rows.pali: Tattha tassa purisassa "maccho"ti sappaṃ gīvāya gahetvā tuṭṭhakālo viya imassāpi yogino āditova attabhāvaṃ paṭilabhitvā tuṭṭhakālo, tassa khippamukhato sīsaṃ nīharitvā sovatthikattayadassanaṃ viya imassa ghanavinibbhogaṃ katvā saṅkhāresu tilakkhaṇadassanaṃ, tassa bhītakālo viya imassa bhayatupaṭṭhānañāṇaṃ.
rows.english: 50.Herein, the time when the meditator was glad at the outset to have acquired a person is like the time when the man was glad to have seized the snake by the neck. This meditator’s seeing the three characteristics in formations after effecting resolution of the compact [into elements] is like the man’s seeing the three marks on pulling the snake’s head out of the mouth of the net. The meditator’s knowledge of appearance as terror is like the time when the man was frightened.

rows.pali: Tato ādīnavadassanaṃ viya ādīnavānupassanāñāṇaṃ, gahaṇe nibbindanaṃ viya nibbidānupassanāñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: Knowledge of contemplation of danger is like the man’s thereupon seeing the danger. Knowledge of contemplation of dispassion is like the man’s dispassion (revulsion) for what he had seized.

rows.pali: Sappaṃ muñcitukāmatā viya muñcitukamyatāñāṇaṃ, muñcanassa upāyakaraṇaṃ viya paṭisaṅkhānupassanāñāṇena saṅkhāresu tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
rows.english: Knowledge of desire for deliverance is like the man’s deliverance from the snake. The attribution of the three characteristics to formations by knowledge of contemplation of reflection is like the man’s contriving a means to deliverance.

rows.pali: Yathā hi so puriso sappaṃ āvijjhitvā dubbalaṃ katvā nivattetvā ḍaṃsituṃ asamatthabhāvaṃ pāpetvā sumuttaṃ muñcati, evamayaṃ yogāvacaro tilakkhaṇāropanena saṅkhāre āvijjhitvā dubbale katvā puna niccasukhasubhaattākārena upaṭṭhātuṃ asamatthataṃ pāpetvā sumuttaṃ muñcati.
rows.english: For just as the man weakened the snake by swinging it, keeping it away and rendering it incapable of biting, and was thus quite delivered, so too this meditator weakens formations by swinging them with the attribution of the three characteristics, rendering them incapable of appearing again in the modes of permanence, pleasure, beauty, and self, and is thus quite delivered.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "muñcanassa upāyasampādanatthaṃ evaṃ pariggaṇhātī"ti.
rows.english: That is why it was said above that he discerns them in this way “in order to contrive the means to deliverance.”

rows.pali: 759.Ettāvatā tassa uppannaṃ hoti paṭisaṅkhāñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: 51.At this point knowledge of reflection has arisen in him,

rows.pali: Yaṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ –
rows.english: with reference to which it is said:

rows.pali: Ettha ca nimittaṃ paṭisaṅkhāti saṅkhāranimittaṃ "addhuvaṃ tāvakālika"nti aniccalakkhaṇavasena jānitvā.
rows.english: 52. As here after reflecting on the sign [means] having known the sign of formations by means of the characteristic of impermanence as unlasting and temporary.

rows.pali: Kāmañca na paṭhamaṃ jānitvā pacchā ñāṇaṃ uppajjati, vohāravasena pana "manañca paṭicca dhamme ca uppajjati manoviññāṇa"ntiādīni (ma. ni. 3.421) viya evaṃ vuccati.
rows.english: Of course, it is not17 that, first having known, subsequently knowledge arises; but it is expressed in this way according to common usage, as in the passage beginning, “Due to (lit. having depended upon) mind and mental object, mind-consciousness arises” (M I 112), and so on.

rows.pali: Ekattanayena vā purimañca pacchimañca ekaṃ katvā evaṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Or alternatively, it can be understood as expressed thus according to the method of identity by identifying the preceding with the subsequent.

rows.pali: Iminā nayena itarasmimpi padadvaye attho veditabboti.
rows.english: The meaning of the remaining two expressions [that is, “occurrence” and “the sign and occurrence”] should be understood in the same way.

rows.pali: 760.So evaṃ paṭisaṅkhānupassanāñāṇena "sabbe saṅkhārā suññā"ti pariggahetvā puna "suññamidaṃ attena vā attaniyena vā"ti (ma. ni. 3.69) dvikoṭikaṃ suññataṃ pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: 53. Having thus discerned by knowledge of contemplation of reflection that “All formations are void” (see S III 167), he again discerns voidness in the double logical relation18 thus: “This is void of self or of what belongs to self” (M II 263; Paṭis II 36).

rows.pali: So evaṃ neva attānaṃ, na paraṃ kiñci attano parikkhārabhāve ṭhitaṃ disvā puna "nāhaṃ kvacani, kassaci kiñcanatasmiṃ, na ca mama kvacani, kismiñci kiñcanatatthī"ti yā ettha catukoṭikā suññatā kathitā, taṃ pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: When he has thus seen that there is neither a self nor any other [thing or being] occupying the position of a self s property, he again discerns voidness in the quadruple logical relation as set forth in this passage: “I am not anywhere anyone’s owning, nor is there anywhere my owning in anyone (nāhaṃ kvacani kassaci kiñcanat’ asmiṃ na ca mama kvacani kismiñci kiñcanat’ atthi)” (M II 263).19

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Ayañhi nāhaṃ kvacanīti kvaci attānaṃ na passati.
rows.english: 54.(i) This [meditator, thinking] I … not anywhere (nāhaṃ kvacani), does not see a self anywhere;

rows.pali: Kassaci kiñcanatasminti attano attānaṃ kassaci parassa kiñcanabhāve upanetabbaṃ na passati.
rows.english: (ii) [Thinking] am … anyone’s owning (kassaci kiñcanat’ asmiṃ), he does not see a self of his own to be inferred in another’s owning;

rows.pali: Bhātiṭṭhānevā bhātaraṃ, sahāyaṭṭhāne vā sahāyaṃ, parikkhāraṭṭhāne vā parikkhāraṃ maññitvā upanetabbaṃ na passatīti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is that he does not see [a self of his own] deducible by conceiving a brother [to own it] in the case of a brother,20 a friend [to own it] in the case of a friend, or a chattel [to own it] in the case of a chattel;

rows.pali: Na ca mama kvacanīti ettha mama-saddaṃ tāva ṭhapetvā na ca kvacanīti parassa ca attānaṃ kvaci napassatīti ayamattho.
rows.english: (iii) [As regards the phrase] nor … anywhere my (na ca mama kvacani), leaving aside the word my (mama) here for the moment, [the words] nor anywhere (na ca kvacani) [means that] he does not21 see another’s self anywhere;

rows.pali: Idāni mama-saddaṃ āharitvā mama kismiñci kiñcanatatthīti so parassa attā mama kismiñci kiñcanabhāve atthīti na passatīti.
rows.english: (iv) Now, bringing in the word my (mama), [we have] is there … my owning in anyone (mama kismiñci kiñcanat’ atthi): he does not see thus, “Another’s self exists owing to some state of my owning22 [of it]”;

rows.pali: Attano bhātiṭṭhāne vā bhātaraṃ, sahāyaṭṭhāne vā sahāyaṃ parikkhāraṭṭhāne vā parikkhāranti kismiñci ṭhāne parassa attānaṃ iminā kiñcanabhāvena upanetabbaṃ na passatīti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is that he does not see in any instance another’s self deducible owing to this fact of his owning a brother in the case of a brother, a friend in the case of a friend, chattel in the case of a chattel.

rows.pali: Evamayaṃ yasmā neva katthaci attānaṃ passati, na taṃ parassa kiñcanabhāve upanetabbaṃ passati, na parassa attānaṃ passati, na parassa attānaṃ attano kiñcanabhāve upanetabbaṃ passati.
rows.english: So (i) he sees no self anywhere [of his own]; (ii) nor does he see it as deducible in the fact of another’s owning; (iii) nor does he see another’s self; (iv) nor does he see that as deducible in the fact of his own owning.23

rows.pali: Tasmānena catukoṭikā suññatā pariggahitā hotīti.
rows.english: This is how he discerns voidness in the quadruple logical relation.

rows.pali: 761.Evaṃ catukoṭikaṃ suññataṃ pariggahetvā puna chahākārehi suññataṃ pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: 55. Having discerned voidness in the quadruple logical relation in this way, he discerns voidness again in six modes.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Cakkhu suññaṃ attena vā attaniyena vā niccena vā dhuvena vā sassatena vā avipariṇāmadhammena vā - pe - mano suñño.
rows.english: Eye (i) is void of self, (ii) or of the property of a self, (iii) or of permanence, (iv) or of lastingness, (v) or of eternalness, (vi) or of non-subjectness to change; … mind …

rows.pali: Rūpā suññā - pe - dhammā suññā.
rows.english: visible data … mental data …

rows.pali: Cakkhuviññāṇaṃ - pe - manoviññāṇaṃ.
rows.english: eye-consciousness … mind-consciousness …

rows.pali: Cakkhusamphassoti evaṃ yāva jarāmaraṇā nayo netabbo.
rows.english: mind-contact … (Nidd II 187 (Se); Nidd II 279 (Ee); cf.S IV 54) and this should be continued as far as ageing-and- death (see XX.9).

rows.pali: 762.Evaṃ chahākārehi suññataṃ pariggahetvā puna aṭṭhahākārehi pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: 56. Having discerned voidness in the six modes in this way, he discerns it again in eight modes,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – rūpaṃ asāraṃ nissāraṃ sārāpagataṃ niccasārasārena vā dhuvasārasārena vā sukhasārasārena vā attasārasārena vā niccena vā dhuvena vā sassatena vā avipariṇāmadhammena vā.
rows.english: that is to say: “Materiality has no core, is coreless, without core, as far as concerns (i) any core of permanence, or (ii) core of lastingness, or (iii) core of pleasure, or (iv) core of self, or as far as concerns (v) what is permanent, or (vi) what is lasting, or (vii) what is eternal, or (viii) what is not subject to change.

rows.pali: Vedanā… saññā… saṅkhārā… viññāṇaṃ… cakkhu - pe - jarāmaraṇaṃ asāraṃ nissāraṃ sārāpagataṃ niccasārasārena vā dhuvasārasārena vā sukhasārasārena vā attasārasārena vā niccena vā dhuvena vā sassatena vā avipariṇāmadhammena vā.
rows.english: Feeling … perception … formations … consciousness … eye … (etc., see XX.9) … ageing-and-death has no core, is coreless, without a core, as far as concerns any core of permanence, or core of lastingness, or core of pleasure, or core of self, or as far as concerns what is permanent, or what is lasting, or what is eternal, or what is not subject to change.

rows.pali: Yathā naḷo asāro nissāro sārāpagato.
rows.english: Just as a reed has no core, is coreless, without core;

rows.pali: Yathā eraṇḍo… yathā udumbaro… yathā setavaccho… yathā pāḷibhaddako… yathā pheṇapiṇḍo… yathā udakabubbuḷaṃ… yathā marīci… yathā kadalikkhandho… yathā māyā asārā nissārā sārāpagatā, evameva rūpaṃ - pe - jarāmaraṇaṃ asāraṃ nissāraṃ sārāpagataṃ niccasārasārena vā - pe - avipariṇāmadhammena vāti (cūḷani. mogharājamāṇavapucchāniddesa 88).
rows.english: just as a castor-oil plant, an udumbara (fig) tree, a setavaccha tree, a pāḷibhaddaka tree, a lump of froth, a bubble on water, a mirage, a plantain trunk, a conjuring trick, has no core, is coreless, without core, so too materiality …(etc) … ageing-and-death has no core … or what is subject to change” (Nidd II 184–85 (Se); Nidd II 278–89 (Ee)).

rows.pali: 763.So evaṃ aṭṭhahākārehi suññataṃ pariggahetvā puna dasahākārehi pariggaṇhāti, rūpaṃ rittato passati.
rows.english: 57.Having discerned voidness in eight modes in this way, he discerns it again in ten modes. How? “He sees materiality as empty,

rows.pali: Tucchato… suññato… anattato… anissariyato… akāmakāriyato… alabbhanīyato… avasavattakato… parato… vivittato passati.
rows.english: as vain, as not-self, as having no Overlord, as incapable of being made into what one wants, as incapable of being had [as one wishes], as insusceptible to the exercise of mastery, as alien, as secluded [from past and future].

rows.pali: Vedanaṃ - pe - viññāṇaṃ rittato - pe - vivittato passatīti.
rows.english: He sees feeling … (etc.) … consciousness as empty, … as secluded”24 (Nidd II 279 (Ee)).

rows.pali: 764.Evaṃ dasahākārehi suññataṃ pariggahetvā puna dvādasahākārehi pariggaṇhāti.
rows.english: 58.Having discerned voidness in ten modes in this way, he discerns it again in twelve modes,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – rūpaṃ na satto, na jīvo, na naro, na māṇavo, na itthī, na puriso, na attā, na attaniyaṃ.
rows.english: that is to say: “Materiality is no living being,25 no soul, no human being, no man, no female, no male, no self, no property of a self,

rows.pali: Nāhaṃ, na mama, na aññassa, na kassaci.
rows.english: not I, not mine, not another’s, not anyone’s.

rows.pali: Vedanā - pe - viññāṇaṃ na kassacīti (cūḷani. mogharājamāṇavapucchāniddesa 88).
rows.english: Feeling … (etc.) … consciousness … not anyone’s (Nidd II 186 (Se); Nidd II 280 (Ee)).

rows.pali: 765.Evaṃ dvādasahākārehi suññataṃ pariggaṇhitvā puna tīraṇapariññāvasena dvācattālīsāya ākārehi suññataṃ pariggaṇhāti, rūpaṃ aniccato… dukkhato… rogato… gaṇḍato… sallato… aghato… ābādhato… parato… palokato… ītito… upaddavato… bhayato… upasaggato… calato… pabhaṅguto… addhuvato… atāṇato… aleṇato… asaraṇato… asaraṇībhūtato… rittato… tucchato… suññato… anattato… anassādato… ādīnavato… vipariṇāmadhammato… assārakato… aghamūlato… vadhakato… vibhavato… sāsavato… saṅkhatato… mārāmisato… jātidhammato… jarādhammato… byādhidhammato… maraṇadhammato… sokaparidevadukkhadomanassaupāyāsadhammato… samudayato… atthaṅgamato… anassādato … ādīnavato… nissaraṇato passati.
rows.english: 59. Having discerned voidness in twelve modes in this way, he discerns it again in forty-two modes through full-understanding as investigating. He sees materiality as impermanent, as painful, as a disease, a tumour, a dart, a calamity, an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, a plague, a disaster, a terror, a menace, as fickle, perishable, unenduring, as no protection, no shelter, no refuge, as unfit to be a refuge, as empty, vain, void, not-self, as without satisfaction,26 as a danger, as subject to change, as having no core, as the root of calamity, as murderous, as due to be annihilated, as subject to cankers, as formed, as Māra’s bait, as subject to birth, subject to ageing, subject to illness, subject to death, subject to sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief and despair; as arising, as departing; as danger,27 as (having an) escape.

rows.pali: Vedanaṃ - pe - viññāṇaṃ aniccato - pe - nissaraṇato passati.
rows.english: He sees feeling … (etc.) … consciousness … as (having an) escape (cf. Paṭis II 238).

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ – "rūpaṃ aniccato - pe - nissaraṇato passanto suññato lokaṃ avekkhati.
rows.english: 60. And this is said too:28 “When he sees materiality as impermanent … as (having an) escape, he looks upon the world as void.

rows.pali: Vedanaṃ - pe - viññāṇaṃ aniccato - pe - nissaraṇato passanto suññato lokaṃ avekkhati".
rows.english: When he sees feeling … (etc.) … consciousness as impermanent … as (having an) escape, he looks upon the world as void.”

rows.pali: 766.Evaṃ suññato disvā tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā saṅkhāre pariggaṇhanto bhayañca nandiñca vippahāya saṅkhāresu udāsīno ahosi majjhatto, ahanti vā mamanti vā na gaṇhāti vissaṭṭhabhariyo viya puriso.
rows.english: 61.When he has discerned formations by attributing the three characteristics to them and seeing them as void in this way, he abandons both terror and delight, he becomes indifferent to them and neutral, he neither takes them as “I” nor as mine,” he is like a man who has divorced his wife.

rows.pali: Yathā nāma purisassa bhariyā bhaveyya iṭṭhā kantā manāpā, so tāya vinā muhuttampi adhivāsetuṃ na sakkuṇeyya, ativiya naṃ mamāyeyya, so taṃ itthiṃ aññena purisena saddhiṃ ṭhitaṃ vā nisinnaṃ vā kathentiṃ vā hasantiṃ vā disvā kupito assa anattamano, adhimattaṃ domanassaṃ paṭisaṃvedeyya.
rows.english: 62.Suppose a man were married to a lovely, desirable, charming wife and so deeply in love with her as to be unable to bear separation from her for a moment. He would be disturbed and displeased to see her standing or sitting or talking or laughing with another man, and would be very unhappy;

rows.pali: So aparena samayena tassā itthiyā dosaṃ disvā muñcitukāmo hutvā taṃ vissajjeyya, na naṃ mamāti gaṇheyya.
rows.english: but later, when he had found out the woman’s faults, and wanting to get free, had divorced her, he would no more take her as “mine”;

rows.pali: Tato paṭṭhāya taṃ yenakenaci saddhiṃ yaṃkiñci kurumānaṃ disvāpi neva kuppeyya, na domanassaṃ āpajjeyya, aññadatthu udāsīnova bhaveyya majjhatto.
rows.english: and thereafter, even though he saw her doing whatever it might be with whomsoever it might be, he would not be disturbed or displeased, but would on the contrary be indifferent and neutral.

rows.pali: Evamevāyaṃ sabbasaṅkhārehi muñcitukāmo hutvā paṭisaṅkhānupassanāya saṅkhāre pariggaṇhanto ahaṃ mamāti gahetabbaṃ adisvā bhayañca nandiñca vippahāya sabbasaṅkhāresu udāsīno hoti majjhatto.
rows.english: So too this [meditator], wanting to get free from all formations, discerns formations by the contemplation of reflection; then, seeing nothing to be taken as “I” or “mine,” he abandons both terror and delight and becomes indifferent and neutral towards all formations.

rows.pali: Tassa evaṃ jānato evaṃ passato tīsu bhavesu catūsu yonīsu pañcasu gatīsu sattasu viññāṇaṭṭhitīsu navasu sattāvāsesu cittaṃ patilīyati patikuṭati pativattati na sampasāriyati, upekkhā vā pāṭikulyatā vā saṇṭhāti.
rows.english: 63.When he knows and sees thus, his heart retreats, retracts and recoils from the three kinds of becoming, the four kinds of generation, the five kinds of destiny, the seven stations of consciousness, and the nine abodes of beings; his heart no longer goes out to them. Either equanimity or repulsiveness is established.

rows.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma padumapalāse īsakapoṇe udakaphusitāni patilīyanti patikuṭanti pativattanti na sampasāriyanti, evameva - pe - seyyathāpi nāma kukkuṭapattaṃ vā nahārudaddulaṃ vā aggimhi pakkhittaṃ patilīyati patikuṭati pativattati na sampasāriyati (a. ni. 7.49), evameva tassa tīsu bhavesu cittaṃ - pe - upekkhā vā pāṭikulyatā vā saṇṭhāti.
rows.english: Just as water drops retreat, retract and recoil on a lotus leaf that slopes a little and do not spread out, so too his heart … And just as a fowl’s feather or a shred of sinew thrown on a fire retreats, retracts and recoils, and does not spread out, so too his heart retreats, retracts and recoils from the three kinds of becoming … Either equanimity or repulsiveness is established.

rows.pali: Iccassa saṅkhārupekkhāñāṇaṃ nāma uppannaṃ hoti.
rows.english: In this way there arises in him what is called knowledge of equanimity about formations.

rows.pali: 767.Taṃ panetaṃ sace santipadaṃ nibbānaṃ santato passati, sabbaṃ saṅkhārappavattaṃ vissajjetvā nibbānameva pakkhandati.
rows.english: 64. But if this [knowledge] sees Nibbāna, the state of peace, as peaceful, it rejects the occurrence of all formations and enters only into Nibbāna.

rows.pali: No ce nibbānaṃ santato passati, punappunaṃ saṅkhārārammaṇameva hutvā pavattati sāmuddikānaṃ disākāko viya.
rows.english: If it does not see Nibbāna as peaceful, it occurs again and again with formations as its object, like the sailors’ crow.

rows.pali: Sāmuddikā kira vāṇijakā nāvaṃ ārohantā disākākaṃ nāma gaṇhanti, te yadā nāvā vātakkhittā videsaṃ pakkhandati, tīraṃ na paññāyati, tadā disākākaṃ vissajjenti.
rows.english: 65.When traders board a ship, it seems, they take with them what is called a land-finding crow. When the ship gets blown off its course by gales and goes adrift with no land in sight, then they release the land-finding crow.

rows.pali: So kūpakayaṭṭhito ākāsaṃ laṅghitvā sabbā disā ca vidisā ca anugantvā sace tīraṃ passati, tadabhimukhova gacchati.
rows.english: It takes off from the mast-head,30 and after exploring all the quarters, if it sees land, it flies straight in the direction of it;

rows.pali: No ce passati, punappunaṃ āgantvā kūpakayaṭṭhiṃyeva allīyati.
rows.english: if not, it returns and alights on the mast-head.

rows.pali: Evameva sace saṅkhārupekkhāñāṇaṃ santipadaṃ nibbānaṃ santato passati, sabbaṃ saṅkhārappavattaṃ vissajjetvā nibbānameva pakkhandati.
rows.english: So too, if knowledge of equanimity about formations sees Nibbāna, the state of peace, as peaceful, it rejects the occurrence of all formations and enters only into Nibbāna.

rows.pali: No ce passati, punappunaṃ saṅkhārārammaṇameva hutvā pavattati.
rows.english: If it does not see it, it occurs again and again with formations as its object.

rows.pali: Tadidaṃ suppagge piṭṭhaṃ vaṭṭayamānaṃ viya.
rows.english: as though sifting flour on the edge of a tray,

rows.pali: Nibbaṭṭitakappāsaṃ vihanamānaṃ viya nānappakārato saṅkhāre pariggahetvā bhayañca nandiñca pahāya saṅkhāravicinane majjhattaṃ hutvā tividhānupassanāvasena tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: 6. Now, after discerning formations in the various modes,6 (as above) as though carding cotton from which the seeds have been picked out,31 and after abandoning terror and delight, and after becoming neutral in the investigation of formations, he still persists in the triple contemplation.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tiṭṭhamānaṃ tividhavimokkhamukhabhāvaṃ āpajjitvā sattaariyapuggalavibhāgāya paccayo hoti.
rows.english: And in so doing, this [insight knowledge] enters upon the state of the triple gateway to liberation, and it becomes a condition for the classification of noble persons into seven kinds.

rows.pali: 768.Tatridaṃ tividhānupassanāvasena pavattanato tiṇṇaṃ indriyānaṃ ādhipateyyavasena tividhavimokkhamukhabhāvaṃ āpajjati nāma.
rows.english: It enters upon the state of the triple gateway to liberation now with the predominance of [one of] three faculties according as the contemplation occurs in [one of] the three ways.32

rows.pali: Tisso hi anupassanā tīṇi vimokkhamukhānīti vuccanti.
rows.english: 67.For it is the three contemplations that are called the three gateways to liberation,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Tattha paricchedaparivaṭumatoti udayabbayavasena paricchedato ceva parivaṭumato ca.
rows.english: 68. Herein, as limited and circumscribed [means] both as limited by rise and fall and as circumscribed by them;

rows.pali: Aniccānupassanaṃ hi "udayato pubbe saṅkhārā natthī"ti paricchinditvā tesaṃ gatiṃ samannesamānaṃ "vayato paraṃ na gacchanti, ettheva antaradhāyantī"ti parivaṭumato samanupassati.
rows.english: for contemplation of impermanence limits them thus, “Formations do not exist previous to their rise,” and in seeking their destiny, sees them as circumscribed thus, “They do not go beyond fall, they vanish there.”

rows.pali: Manosamuttejanatāyāti cittasaṃvejanatāya.
rows.english: To the stirring up of the mind: by giving consciousness a sense of urgency;

rows.pali: Dukkhānupassanena hi saṅkhāresu cittaṃ saṃvejeti.
rows.english: for with the contemplation of pain consciousness acquires a sense of urgency with respect to formations.

rows.pali: Parato samanupassanatāyāti "nāhaṃ, na mamā"ti evaṃ anattato samanupassanatāya.
rows.english: To the seeing … as alien: to contemplating them as not- self thus: “Not I,” “Not mine.”

rows.pali: Iti imāni tīṇi padāni aniccānupassanādīnaṃ vasena vuttānīti veditabbāni.
rows.english: 69.So these three clauses should be understood to express the contemplations of impermanence, and so on.

rows.pali: Teneva tadanantare pañhavissajjane vuttaṃ – "aniccato manasikaroto khayato saṅkhārā upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: Hence in the answer to the next question [asked in the Paṭisambhidā] it is said: “When he brings [them] to mind as impermanent, formations appear as liable to destruction.

rows.pali: Dukkhato manasikaroto bhayato saṅkhārā upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: When he brings them to mind as painful, formations appear as a terror.

rows.pali: Anattato manasikaroto suññato saṅkhārā upaṭṭhahantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 1.219).
rows.english: When he brings them to mind as not-self, formations appear as void” (Paṭis II 48).

rows.pali: 769.Katame pana te vimokkhā, yesaṃ imāni anupassanāni mukhānīti?
rows.english: 70.What are the liberations to which these contemplations are the gateways?

rows.pali: Animitto, appaṇihito, suññatoti ete tayo.
rows.english: They are these three, namely, the signless, the desireless, and the void.

rows.pali: Vuttaṃ hetaṃ "aniccato manasikaronto adhimokkhabahulo animittaṃ vimokkhaṃ paṭilabhati.
rows.english: For this is said: “When one who has great resolution brings [formations] to mind as impermanent, he acquires the signless liberation.

rows.pali: Dukkhato manasikaronto passaddhibahulo appaṇihitaṃ vimokkhaṃ paṭilabhati.
rows.english: When one who has great tranquillity brings [them] to mind as painful, he acquires the desireless liberation.

rows.pali: Anattato manasikaronto vedabahulo suññatavimokkhaṃ paṭilabhatī"ti (paṭi. ma. 1.223).
rows.english: When one who has great wisdom brings [them] to mind as not-self, he acquires the void liberation” (Paṭis II 58).

rows.pali: Ettha ca animitto vimokkhoti animittākārena nibbānaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā pavatto ariyamaggo.
rows.english: 71.And here the signless liberation should be understood as the noble path that has occurred by making Nibbāna its object through the signless aspect.

rows.pali: So hi animittāya dhātuyā uppannattā animitto.
rows.english: For that path is signless owing to the signless element having arisen,

rows.pali: Kilesehi ca vimuttattā vimokkho.
rows.english: and it is a liberation owing to deliverance from defilements.34

rows.pali: Eteneva nayena appaṇihitākārena nibbānaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā pavatto appaṇihito.
rows.english: In the same way the path that has occurred by making Nibbāna its object through the desireless aspect is desireless.

rows.pali: Suññatākārena nibbānaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā pavatto suññatoti veditabbo.
rows.english: And the path that has occurred by making Nibbāna its object through the void aspect is void.

rows.pali: 770.Yaṃ pana abhidhamme "yasmiṃ samaye lokuttaraṃ jhānaṃ bhāveti niyyānikaṃ apacayagāmiṃ diṭṭhigatānaṃ pahānāya paṭhamāya bhūmiyā pattiyā vivicceva kāmehi paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati appaṇihitaṃ suññata"nti (dha. sa. 343 ādayo) evaṃ vimokkhadvayameva vuttaṃ, taṃ nippariyāyato vipassanāgamanaṃ sandhāya.
rows.english: 72.But it is said in the Abhidhamma: “On the occasion when he develops the supramundane jhāna that is an outlet and leads to dispersal, having abandoned the field of [false] views with the reaching of the first grade, secluded from sense desires he enters upon and dwells in the first jhāna, which is desireless … is void,” (Dhs §510) thus mentioning only two liberations. This refers to the way in which insight arrives [at the path] and is expressed literally.

rows.pali: Vipassanāñāṇaṃ hi kiñcāpi paṭisambhidāmagge –
rows.english: 73.However, in the Paṭisambhidā insight knowledge is expressed as follows:

rows.pali: Vuttaṃ.
rows.english: it is expressed (above).

rows.pali: Tathāpi taṃ saṅkhāranimittassa avijahanato na nippariyāyena animittaṃ.
rows.english: But although stated in this way, insight knowledge is not literally signless because there is no abandoning of the sign of formations [as formed, here, as distinct from their sign as impermanent and so on].

rows.pali: Nippariyāyena pana suññatañceva appaṇihitañca.
rows.english: It is however literally void and desireless.

rows.pali: Tassa ca āgamanavasena ariyamaggakkhaṇe vimokkho uddhaṭo.
rows.english: And it is at the moment of the noble path that the liberation is distinguished, and that is done according to insight knowledge’s way of arrival at the path.35

rows.pali: Tasmā appaṇihitaṃ suññatanti vimokkhadvayameva vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: That, it should be understood, is why only two liberations are stated [in the Abhidhamma], namely, the desireless and the void.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tāvettha vimokkhakathā.
rows.english: This, firstly, is the treatise on the liberations here.

rows.pali: 771.Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "sattaariyapuggalavibhāgāya paccayo hotī"ti, tattha saddhānusārī, saddhāvimutto, kāyasakkhi, ubhatobhāgavimutto, dhammānusārī, diṭṭhippatto, paññāvimuttoti ime tāva satta ariyapuggalā, tesaṃ vibhāgāya idaṃ saṅkhārupekkhāñāṇaṃ paccayo hoti.
rows.english: 74. It was said above, “It becomes a condition for the classification of noble persons into seven kinds.” (§66) Herein, there are firstly these seven kinds of noble person: (1) the faith devotee, (2) one liberated by faith, (3) the body witness, (4) the both-ways liberated, (5) the Dhamma devotee, (6) one attained to vision, and (7) one liberated by understanding. This knowledge of equanimity about formations is a condition for their being placed as these seven classes.

rows.pali: 772.Yo hi aniccato manasikaronto adhimokkhabahulo saddhindriyaṃ paṭilabhati, so sotāpattimaggakkhaṇe saddhānusārī hoti.
rows.english: 75. When a man brings [formations] to mind as impermanent and, having great resolution, acquires the faith faculty, (1) he becomes a faith devotee at the moment of the stream-entry path;

rows.pali: Sesesu sattasu ṭhānesu saddhāvimutto.
rows.english: and in the other seven instances [that is, in the three higher paths and the four fruitions] he becomes (2) one liberated by faith.

rows.pali: 773.Yo pana dukkhato manasikaronto passaddhibahulo samādhindriyaṃ paṭilabhati, so sabbattha kāyasakkhi nāma hoti.
rows.english: When a man brings [them] to mind as painful and, having great tranquillity, acquires the faculty of concentration, (3) he is called a body witness in all eight instances.

rows.pali: Arūpajjhānaṃ pana patvā aggaphalappatto ubhatobhāgavimutto nāma hoti.
rows.english: (4) He is called both-ways liberated when he has reached the highest fruition after also reaching the immaterial jhānas.

rows.pali: 774.Yo pana anattato manasikaronto vedabahulo paññindriyaṃ paṭilabhati, so sotāpattimaggakkhaṇe dhammānusārī hoti.
rows.english: When a man brings [them] to mind as not-self and, having great wisdom, acquires the faculty of understanding, he becomes (5) a Dhamma devotee at the moment of the stream-entry path;

rows.pali: Chasu ṭhānesu diṭṭhippatto aggaphale paññāvimuttoti.
rows.english: (6) in the next six instances he becomes one attained to vision; and (7) in the case of the highest fruition he becomes one liberated by understanding.

rows.pali: 775.Vuttaṃ hetaṃ –
rows.english: 76. (1) This is said:

rows.pali: 776.Aparampi vuttaṃ –
rows.english: 77.It is said further:

rows.pali: "Saddahanto vimuttoti saddhāvimutto.
rows.english: “[At the moment of the first path:] (2) he has been liberated by having faith (saddahanto vimutto), thus he is one liberated by faith;

rows.pali: Phuṭṭhantaṃ sacchikatoti kāyasakkhi.
rows.english: (3) he has realized [Nibbāna] by experiencing, thus he is a body witness;

rows.pali: Diṭṭhantaṃ pattoti diṭṭhippatto.
rows.english: (6) he has attained [Nibbāna] by vision, thus he is one attained to vision.

rows.pali: Saddahanto vimuccatīti saddhāvimutto.
